does: This paper proposes POP, a self-play framework that extends self-play training to open-ended tasks where objective ground truth is unavailable. The core innovation is having a single LLM generate three components: task inputs, outputs, and evaluation rubrics that guide assessment of those outputs. The rubric is derived from patterns in a pre-training corpus, grounding the evaluation in realistic content. The framework uses these rubric-based evaluations as reward signals to train the LLM via reinforcement learning, eliminating the need for human-annotated input-output pairs or expensive proprietary models. The approach bootstraps post-training signals entirely from the model's own generations and the pre-training text, making self-play viable for subjective domains like writing, reasoning, and creative tasks where previous self-play methods failed.
connects: This work builds on recent self-play paradigms for LLM post-training, which have proven effective for verifiable tasks with checkable ground truth like mathematics and code generation. It addresses a key limitation of prior self-play work by Zhu et al., Silver et al., and others who focused exclusively on tasks admitting objective evaluation. The paper extends classical self-play concepts from game-playing and reinforcement learning to language modeling. It connects to broader research on reward modeling for LLMs and the challenge of learning from weak or synthetic supervision. The rubric-based evaluation approach draws on ideas from evaluation metrics in NLP and connects to work on using language models to provide self-generated feedback, building on recent findings that LLMs can serve as evaluators when properly guided.
enables: This framework opens self-play training to a much broader class of open-ended language tasks, enabling post-training of LLMs without costly human annotation. It points toward fully automated post-training pipelines where models improve themselves through iterative self-generation and self-evaluation. The work could enable scaling post-training to diverse domains like creative writing, dialogue, summarization, and complex reasoning where ground truth evaluation is inherently subjective. Future research could investigate how to improve rubric quality and diversity, combine multiple evaluation signals, apply this approach to multi-turn interactions, or extend the framework to collaborative multi-agent self-play. The grounding in pre-training corpus content suggests applications to specializing models for specific domains or knowledge bases with minimal additional supervision, and demonstrates a path toward more autonomous model improvement.
